items.title: Typical age of presentation
items.contentHtml:
Typical age of presentation (2-8 weeks)

  • Hypertrophic pyloric stenosis classically presents between 2 and 8 weeks of life, with a maximum peak in week 3-5.
  • It is exceptional before the first week or after the third month; if it occurs, reconsider alternative diagnoses such as gastroesophageal reflux or intestinal malrotation.
  • Age is a key piece of information in the examination: they ask about the typical range and the trick is to confuse it with vomiting of the newborn (first days) or with late vomiting (>12 weeks).

items.title: Non-bilious projectile vomiting
items.contentHtml:
Non-bilious projectile vomiting

  • The vomiting is projectile, non-bilious (absence of bile due to obstruction proximal to the ampulla of Vater), and occurs immediately after feeding.
  • The child remains hungry and begins to suck avidly again; This differentiates it from gastroesophageal reflux where the vomiting is more regurgitant and not explosive.
  • The absence of bile is essential: if there is bile, think about distal obstruction (e.g., duodenal atresia, malrotation with volvulus).

items.title: Palpable olive sign
items.contentHtml:
Palpable olive sign

  • The olive sign consists of palpating a firm, mobile mass, the size of an olive, in the right hypochondrium (epigastrium), corresponding to the hypertrophic pylorus.
  • It is best palpated after emptying the stomach with a nasogastric tube and with the child relaxed (or during suction).
  • The sensitivity of palpation is 50-80% and depends on the experience of the examiner; Ultrasound has displaced it as the diagnostic standard.
  • During the examination, they usually ask: “What clinical sign is pathognomonic?” Answer: palpable pyloric olive.

items.foco: Peristalsis visible from left to right
items.title: Visible peristalsis
items.contentHtml:
Peristalsis visible from left to right

  • Gastric peristaltic waves traveling from left to right (from the left hypochondrium to the epigastrium) are observed due to the contraction of the stomach against the obstructed pylorus.
  • They are more evident after feeding and in thin children; they may precede vomiting.
  • It is not pathognomonic, but it is a classic finding; its absence does not rule out the diagnosis.
  • On differential examination, visible peristalsis can also be seen in duodenal atresia, but there the vomiting is bilious.

items.title: Hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis
items.contentHtml:
Hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis

  • Due to loss of hydrochloric acid in vomit, hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis occurs (pH >7.45, high bicarbonate, low serum chloride <100 mEq/L).
  • The kidney compensates with potassium excretion (hypokalemia) and sodium retention, aggravating the alkalosis. paradoxical: acid urine in the context of systemic alkalosis.
  • In severe cases, there may be ionized hypocalcemia due to alkalemia, with the risk of tetany.
  • Correction of the imbalance is a priority before surgery: 0.9% saline solution with dextrose and potassium replacement.

items.foco: Ultrasound as a diagnostic test of choice
items.title: Diagnostic ultrasound of choice
items.contentHtml:
Ultrasound as the diagnostic test of choice

  • Abdominal ultrasound is the test of choice due to its high sensitivity (close to 100%) and specificity, without radiation.
  • It is performed with a high-frequency linear transducer, ideally after 4-6 hours of fasting and with the child in the supine or right lateral position.
  • It allows measuring the thickness and length of the pylorus, and visualize the image in a “donut” or “ring” in cross section.
  • In the examination, they ask: “what is the diagnostic gold standard?” Answer: abdominal ultrasound.

items.foco: Measurement of thickness and length of the pylorus
items.title: Ultrasound measurement of the pylorus
items.contentHtml:
Measurement of the thickness and length of the pylorus

  • The diagnostic ultrasound criteria are: thickness of the muscle wall >3 mm (measured from the serosa to the mucosa) and length of the pyloric canal >15-17 mm.
  • A thickness <2 mm rules out the disease; Values ​​between 2-3 mm are a gray area and require clinical correlation or repetition.
  • The measurement is performed in the longitudinal axis of the pylorus; It is considered positive if it meets both criteria or at least one very marked one.
  • In the examination, the trap is to confuse it with pyloric spasm, where the thickness is normal but the length may be slightly increased.

items.title: Ramstedt pyloromyotomy
items.contentHtml:
Surgical treatment: Ramstedt pyloromyotomy

  • The definitive treatment is Ramstedt pyloromyotomy, which consists of the longitudinal incision of the muscular layer of the pylorus up to the submucosa, without suturing the mucosa.
  • It is performed by open approach (supraumbilical laparotomy) or laparoscopic, with equal effectiveness and shorter hospital stay in the laparoscopic.
  • Surgery is not urgent; The child must first be stabilized with hydroelectrolyte correction.
  • Complications: mucosal perforation (recognize and suture), hemorrhage, and incomplete incision (persistence of obstruction).

items.title: Pre-surgical hydroelectrolyte correction
items.contentHtml:
Pre-surgical correction of hydroelectrolyte imbalances

  • Before surgery, hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis should be corrected with 0.9% saline plus 5-10% dextrose and potassium replacement (2-4 mEq/kg/day) once diuresis is established.
  • The goal is to normalize chloride. serum (>100 mEq/L) and pH (<7.45) to reduce the risk of postoperative apnea due to depression of the respiratory center.
  • It is administered in boluses of 10-20 mL/kg of 0.9% saline if there is dehydration, and then proceed to maintenance with additional contributions.
  • No operation should be performed until the electrolytes are within safe ranges; Surgery can be delayed 24-48 hours if necessary.

items.foco: Association with male sex and first child
items.title: Epidemiological factors
items.contentHtml:
Association with male sex and first child

  • Hypertrophic pyloric stenosis has a clear predominance in male sex (4:1 to 5:1 compared to women).
  • It is more common in first child (firstborn) and in children of mothers who had the same condition in childhood (increased risk 10-20 times).
  • It is also associated with blood group O and white race (higher incidence in Caucasians).
  • In the exam, they ask: “what is the typical profile?” Answer: male infant, first-born, 3-5 weeks old.

items.foco: Erythromycin intake as a risk factor
items.title: Erythromycin as a risk factor
items.contentHtml:
Erythromycin intake as a risk factor

  • Exposure to erythromycin (macrolide) in the first 2 weeks of life increases the risk of developing hypertrophic pyloric stenosis up to 8-10 times.
  • The mechanism is by prokinetic effect: it stimulates motilin receptors, causing sustained pyloric contractions and hypertrophy muscle.
  • It has also been described with maternal use of erythromycin during breastfeeding or late pregnancy, although with less evidence.
  • During the examination, the question is usually asked: “which antibiotic is associated as a risk factor?” Answer: erythromycin.

items.title: Differential diagnosis with reflux
items.contentHtml:
Differential diagnosis with gastroesophageal reflux

  • Gastroesophageal reflux is the main differential diagnosis: non-projectile vomiting, regurgitation, occur after feeding but not explosive, and the child is not hungry.
  • In reflux there is no palpable olive or visible peristaltic waves, and the ultrasound shows a normal pylorus (thickness <2 mm).
  • Reflux responds to postural and thickening measures; hypertrophic pyloric stenosis requires surgery.
  • Other differentials: duodenal stenosis (bilious vomiting), malrotation with volvulus (bilious vomiting, distention, signs of shock), gastroenteritis (diarrhea, fever).
